one_line_claim: LongMemEval-V2 introduces a 451-question benchmark for long-term web-agent experience memory and shows that a file-based Codex retrieval scaffold, AgentRunbook-C, beats RAG and vanilla Codex on accuracy, but remains slow.
what_they_tested: Memory modules ingest trajectory haystacks of 100 or roughly 500 web-agent runs, return compact text/image evidence, and a fixed Qwen3.5-9B reader answers under a 200K-token truncation budget. They compare no retrieval, query-to-slice RAG, slice-plus-notes RAG, AgentRunbook-R with raw-state/event/note pools and ablations, vanilla Codex, and AgentRunbook-C with workflow, manifest, and helper-script ablations.
strongest_point: The benchmark construction is concrete and useful: manually curated questions span static recall, dynamic state tracking, workflows, gotchas, and premise awareness; haystacks are genuinely large and sparse; and the pilot studies check both no-context failure and oracle-evidence difficulty before presenting the main memory results.
weakest_point: The headline result is reader- and harness-mediated, not a direct demonstration of robust deployed memory. AgentRunbook-C's advantage may partly be a win for expensive query-time coding-agent search over well-packaged synthetic files, while insertion/indexing cost, token/API cost, end-to-end task success, and generalization beyond these sandbox web environments are not the main measured quantities.
missing_baselines: A strong sparse or hybrid retrieval baseline such as BM25/SPLADE plus dense retrieval over state slices; many questions are UI-label and entity heavy.
Dense retrieval with cross-encoder or LLM reranking under the same 200K reader-context budget.
Reasonably adapted existing memory systems such as Mem0, A-MEM, MemGPT-style controllers, Reflexion/workflow-memory methods, or LlamaIndex-style hierarchical retrieval.
Other coding/tool agents at matched budgets, e.g. Claude Code, Gemini CLI, OpenHands, or SWE-agent-like harnesses, to separate the file-search idea from Codex-specific behavior.
End-to-end web-agent task success on held-out tasks with and without the memory modules, not only context gathering for a fixed reader.
Cost-normalized baselines that include query latency, insertion latency, generated-token cost, embedding cost, and storage.
missing_ablations: Report insertion/indexing latency and token cost for event generation, note generation, embedding, manifest construction, and file materialization.
Swap the fixed reader and the GPT-5.2 semantic judge, and test deterministic decoding, to see whether method rankings survive evaluation-model changes.
Separate screenshot-only, accessibility-tree-only, and combined evidence settings; the benchmark is framed as multimodal but some answers may be text-structure recoverable.
Stress-test shared versus question-specific haystacks, since the medium tier uses roughly 500 question-specific trajectories rather than one persistent memory shared across all future queries.
Vary evidence sparsity, number of answer-bearing trajectories, and haystack size to show scaling behavior instead of only small and medium tiers.
Measure prompt and instruction sensitivity for Codex and AgentRunbook-C, since the Codex baseline instruction was manually tuned.
Use model-blind human-only coverage maps or a non-Codex labeling assistant to test whether Codex-assisted labeling advantages Codex-family retrieval agents.
red_flags: The data and evaluation pipeline are entangled with the same family of tools used by the strongest method: trajectories come mostly from GPT-5-mini/GPT-5.2, some collection and answer-trajectory labeling use Codex, and AgentRunbook-C is also Codex-based.

Questions were filtered, rewritten, or perturbed until strong proprietary models failed; this is defensible for preventing parametric leakage but may create an adversarial, trivia-heavy distribution rather than a natural memory workload.
The main score depends on a fixed Qwen3.5-9B reader and GPT-5.2 judges for gotchas/abstention, so retrieval quality and reader presentation quality are not cleanly separated.
UNKNOWN is always counted as incorrect, which makes calibration hard to evaluate and pushes systems toward retrieving something even when evidence is weak.
Reported latency is query latency; AgentRunbook-R does substantial LLM work at insertion time for events and notes, so the accuracy-latency frontier is incomplete without build cost.
AgentRunbook-C still takes about 108-140 seconds per query in the main table, which is more like offline evidence search than an interactive agent memory module.

Only 451 questions across synthetic WebArena/WorkArena-derived environments are tested; the paper acknowledges it does not cover coding agents, computer-use agents, real enterprise data, online learning, or direct task execution.
positive_signals: The paper clearly defines five memory abilities and includes premise-awareness questions, which are often missing from agent-memory benchmarks.
The haystacks are much larger than typical memory benchmarks: about 25M tokens in small and 115M tokens in medium, with sparse answer-bearing trajectories.
Question construction and answer-trajectory coverage include human validation, screenshot-visible evidence requirements, and multi-hop evidence rules.
The no-context and oracle-evidence pilot studies are useful sanity checks: no-context models are near chance, while even oracle trajectories are not trivial for readers.
The authors include meaningful ablations for AgentRunbook-R and AgentRunbook-C rather than only a single benchmark number.
They report latency and analyze error types/tool-calling behavior, which makes the paper more practically informative than a pure leaderboard result.
The limitations and ethics section is relatively candid about synthetic environments, pre-collected trajectories, reader-mediated evaluation, and deployment risks.
verdict: Queue for Paper Radio. This is a strong benchmark-and-systems paper with a timely framing: what does it mean for an agent to become an experienced colleague? Treat the AgentRunbook-C result as evidence that tool-using file search is a strong memory-retrieval controller, not as proof that long-term agent memory is solved. The best discussion angle is the tension between useful direct memory evaluation and the confounds introduced by synthetic tasks, fixed readers, LLM judges, Codex-heavy tooling, and high query latency.
